---
title: "Case Study: Carbon Capture in Synthetic Biology"
---

Carbon capture is the primary worked example for this project. It refers to using engineered
microorganisms to fix atmospheric CO₂ — for example, by optimising RuBisCO activity,
engineering the Calvin cycle, or producing carbon-neutral biofuels. This subfield is
large enough to find examples in both iGEM projects and academic papers, and is directly
relevant to climate policy.

::: {.callout-note}
## What counts as "carbon capture" here?
An artifact is flagged as carbon-capture if its title or abstract contains at least one of:
*carbon capture · carbon sequestration · CO₂ fixation · carbon dioxide reduction ·
carbon neutral · biofuel · carbon cycle · autotrophic · RuBisCO · Calvin cycle · carboxylase ·
carbon concentrating mechanism · photorespiration.*

The keyword list was designed conservatively to avoid over-inclusion of tangential content.
:::

## Carbon-capture corpus snapshot

| Artifact type | Count | Year range |
|---------------|------:|:----------:|
| Papers | 234 | 2005–2026 |
| iGEM projects | 141 | 2011–2025 |
| **Total** | **375** | **2005–2026** |

Carbon-capture artifacts make up **2.7%** of papers and **3.1%** of projects in the full corpus.

---

## Where carbon-capture artifacts sit in semantic space

The figure below shows only the 375 carbon-capture flagged artifacts, coloured by the
cluster they belong to. Most fall into clusters related to metabolic engineering, biofuels,
and photosynthesis — consistent with the keyword filter. A small number appear in adjacent
clusters (e.g. biosensors, DNA assembly), reflecting the interdisciplinary nature of
carbon-capture research.

```{=html}
<iframe src="assets/figures/fig2_umap_cc.html"
        width="100%" height="620px" frameborder="0"
        style="border-radius:4px; box-shadow: 0 1px 4px rgba(0,0,0,0.15);">
</iframe>
```

Carbon-capture artifacts do **not** form a single isolated region in semantic space.
Instead they appear across several clusters that are geographically adjacent in the
UMAP projection. This is expected: carbon capture intersects many synthetic biology
subfields (metabolic engineering, cell-free systems, genetic circuits for biosensing).

---

## Activity over time

The figure below shows the number of carbon-capture papers and iGEM projects per year,
aggregated across all cities. Use the dropdown to zoom in on individual cities.

```{=html}
<iframe src="assets/figures/fig4_cc_timeline.html"
        width="100%" height="520px" frameborder="0"
        style="border-radius:4px; box-shadow: 0 1px 4px rgba(0,0,0,0.15);">
</iframe>
```

Carbon-capture activity in iGEM has grown steadily since around 2015, tracking the
broader rise of climate-focused synthetic biology. Academic papers in the carbon-capture
subfield were present earlier (from 2005) and show a sharper uptick post-2018,
coinciding with increased policy attention to net-zero targets.

---

## City-level carbon-capture overlap

For cities with at least 2 carbon-capture papers **and** at least 2 carbon-capture projects,
we computed a carbon-capture specific semantic overlap score — the cosine similarity between
the centroid of CC papers and the centroid of CC projects in that city.

Only 7 cities meet the minimum document threshold (2 of each type):

| City | Country | CC Papers | CC Projects | CC Semantic Overlap |
|------|---------|----------:|------------:|--------------------:|
| San Diego | US | 3 | 3 | 0.963 |
| Wuhan | CN | 2 | 5 | 0.959 |
| Beijing | CN | 10 | 2 | 0.956 |
| Shanghai | CN | 2 | 6 | 0.948 |
| Guangzhou | CN | 2 | 2 | 0.935 |
| Madison | US | 3 | 2 | 0.929 |
| Austin | US | 2 | 2 | 0.923 |

The small sample (7 cities) means these results should be interpreted with caution.
That said, the overlap scores for these cities are consistently high (0.92–0.96), and
they include expected hubs: Beijing and Shanghai are major synthetic biology centres;
San Diego and Madison host strong biotechnology research communities.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Limitation: small CC sample
The carbon-capture subset is too small for a meaningful regression. With only 7 qualifying
cities, any statistical test would be severely underpowered. The results above are descriptive only.
:::

---

## Interpretation

Three observations from the carbon-capture case study:

**1. Geographic concentration.** The 7 cities with sufficient carbon-capture activity in
both papers and projects are split between the US and China — the two largest synthetic
biology research ecosystems. This reflects the broader corpus, not anything specific to
carbon capture.

**2. High semantic overlap.** Carbon-capture papers and projects in these cities are
topically close (0.92–0.96), comparable to or slightly above the corpus-wide mean (0.94).
This is consistent with the idea that cities engaging in carbon capture iGEM work tend
to also produce academic research in the same specific area.

**3. No temporal precedence claim.** We cannot determine from this cross-sectional data
whether student projects preceded academic output, or vice versa. Both could reflect a
city's pre-existing specialisation in bioenergy or climate biotechnology.
